sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Eye irrigation is the first-line treatment for chemical eye exposure and is also useful for flushing loose superficial contamination. In chemical injuries, early copious irrigation is more important than perfect setup, and it should begin as soon as safely possible.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Use PPE and protect yourself from splash or secondary contamination

2. Start irrigation immediately for chemical exposure, using saline or clean water if saline is not yet available

3. Position the patient so fluid drains away from the unaffected eye and face as much as possible

4. Hold the lids open if needed and direct the irrigant from the inner to outer canthus

5. Continue copious irrigation until symptoms improve, gross contamination is removed, or local guidance for pH reassessment is met

6. Do not attempt to remove embedded foreign bodies; protect the eye and transport

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not delay chemical irrigation for triage, paperwork, or prolonged examination
- Alkali exposures are especially dangerous and often require prolonged irrigation
- Do not apply pressure if open globe injury is suspected
- Document the substance if known, time of exposure, duration of irrigation, and vision if assessable
